data.title: Hong Kong's stablecoins have taken their first step, but the real competition has only just begun.
data.description:
Author: Charlie Liu, Partner, Generative Ventures

Over the past year, although I've been based in the US, I've been deeply involved in the Hong Kong stablecoin process: I served as an advisor to an applicant institution, and was interviewed by Nikkei as a result.

Over the past few months, I've read many comments and analyses, talked to many people, and written several of my own judgments.

Last week, the first batch of licenses was finally issued, and the dust settled. Of course, I have mixed feelings.

Regardless of the outcome, this first step has finally been taken.

I.With the first batch of licenses issued, Hong Kong's first priority is to address "credible issuance"

For Hong Kong, a place that is accustomed to first considering the risks and then allowing the market to grow slowly, the fact that the first step can be implemented is more important than many beautiful narratives.

However, the first step is never the complete answer. On April 10th, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) officially issued the first batch of stablecoin issuer licenses to Anchorpoint Financial, led by Standard Chartered, and HSBC. A total of 36 companies applied in the first batch, but only 2 were approved. The HKMA's standards were very clear: risk control and compliance capabilities, a feasible business plan, a clear use case, and the principle of "similar activities, similar risks, similar regulations." This outcome may not be the most exciting for the market, but it almost perfectly reflects Hong Kong's institutional style. Many people see stablecoins as a trendier wallet product or a more exciting Web3 theme. However, after working with the HKMA, it becomes clear that regulators don't see it that romantically. In the eyes of regulators, stablecoins are primarily a new type of liability instrument, linked to reserves, redemption, anti-money laundering, payments, clearing, and consumer protection. This is precisely why the first batch of licensed institutions will naturally favor those least prone to error, most trustworthy, and most capable of undertaking bank-level regulation. Therefore, if we must first answer "why were the first batch of licenses given to large banks," the answer isn't so dramatic. It's not because stablecoins inherently belong to banks, but because in the early stages of the system, banks best fit the regulatory logic of "stability first, then expansion." The initial issuance of licenses addresses a safe start, not the final stage of the industry. The truly interesting question is never "why banks first," but rather "what kind of ecosystem will emerge in Hong Kong after banks enter the market first." From this perspective, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) has essentially written the question itself. Its initial focus on key scenarios includes not only cross-border and local payments, but also tokenized asset trading, collateral management, and programmable payments. In other words, what Hong Kong issued this time is not just a license to "do payments," but an access permit to enter on-chain financial infrastructure. This difference is crucial. Because once you shift your perspective from "payments" to "on-chain finance," many seemingly disparate things suddenly become connected. Let's first look at the statements from the two licensed institutions themselves. Anchorpoint, led by Standard Chartered, publicly stated that HKDAP is planned to be launched in phases starting from the second quarter of 2026, adopting a B2B2C model and entering the public market through authorized distributors. Its first roadmap also includes cross-border capital and payment flows, as well as the settlement and distribution of tokenized real-world assets. HSBC has also stated that it will launch a Hong Kong dollar stablecoin in the second half of 2026 and plans to integrate it with PayMe and the HSBC HK App, initially covering P2P, merchant payments, and tokenized investments. In other words, even the issuer itself didn't interpret this as simply "issuing one Hong Kong dollar first," but rather actively emphasized distribution, settlement, and asset transfer. This confirms my previous assessment that Hong Kong stablecoins don't need to compete with US dollar stablecoins for global dominance, nor do they need to replicate the path taken by the United States. Its more appropriate position is as the landing layer of Hong Kong's local currency within the on-chain financial system, the "last link" after the US dollar stablecoin completes its cross-border journey, and the most natural settlement currency when Hong Kong local assets enter the on-chain circulation. The first batch of licenses was issued today, and this judgment remains unchanged; in fact, it's become clearer. The only change is that what was previously a structural judgment is now entering the implementation phase. II. Referencing the US Ecosystem: Stablecoins are not payment plugins, but rather the cash leg of on-chain finance. To understand how to judge the implementation phase, the best reference point is not Hong Kong itself, but the United States. Because the stablecoin story that has truly taken off in the US today is no longer just about "paying for a cup of coffee with stablecoins." In July 2025, the GENIUS Act was signed into law, initially incorporating stablecoin payments into the federal institutional framework. By April 2026, White House research documents had explicitly discussed its impact on the banking system based on 1:1 reserves, the scope of eligible assets, and limits on stablecoin yields. Meanwhile, the broader digital asset market structure legislation, the CLARITY Act, is still progressing, but it remains stuck between the banking and crypto industries regarding stablecoin yields/rewards. The first issue the US addressed was "how to formally integrate payment stablecoins into the dollar system"; a larger rewriting of the market structure is still underway. However, the market didn't wait for all the legislation to be finalized before taking action. Following its acquisition of Bridge, Stripe launched Stablecoin Financial Accounts in 2025, allowing businesses in 101 countries to directly hold dollar-denominated stablecoin account balances and move funds between the stablecoin and traditional financial systems. In the same year, Stripe launched Open Issuance through Bridge, allowing companies to issue and manage their own stablecoins. The most important point here is not that "Stripe supports stablecoins," but that Stripe has advanced stablecoins from a payment method to the account, treasury, and product layers. Coinbase took a different, more representative path. It merged the original Coinbase Commerce into Coinbase Business, and deeply recognized that today, businesses are truly using stablecoins not just for receiving payments, but for a combination of custody, fiat offramps, payouts, accounting integrations, and payments. Taking it a step further, Coinbase launched x402 in 2025, sparking the narrative of aggregator commerce by placing stablecoin payments directly at the HTTP layer, allowing APIs, apps, and AI agents to natively complete small payments. Seeing this, it becomes clear that the discussion in the US today is no longer about whether "crypto payments can succeed," but rather whether stablecoins can become the underlying cash layer for internet-native transactions and business operations. Visa's actions also illustrate this point. Instead of becoming a new issuer itself, it continues to embed stablecoins into its core settlement network. In December 2025, Visa announced the launch of USDC settlement in the United States and disclosed that its annualized stablecoin settlement volume had exceeded $3.5 billion. Currently, through deep cooperation with Stripe and Bridge, and by joining the x402 Foundation, Visa has also incorporated stablecoins and the AI ​​payment ecosystem based on stablecoins into its network of networks. Stablecoins often do not replace the old network, but rather rewrite the form of currency circulating in the old network. The network itself may not be disrupted; what is rewritten first is the monetary layer. Looking deeper, payments aren't even the most interesting part of the US stablecoin ecosystem. The truly significant changes are on the asset side. In March 2024, BlackRock launched BUIDL, placing money market fund shares on its public blockchain. Franklin Templeton's Franklin OnChain U.S. Government Money Fund had assets of approximately $844 million by the end of March 2026, with at least 99.5% invested in U.S. government securities, cash, and repurchase agreements fully collateralized by U.S. government securities or cash. DTCC, after receiving a no-action letter from the SEC in December 2025, plans to launch a tokenization service for DTC-custodied real-world assets in the second half of 2026, covering the Russell 1000, major index ETFs, and U.S. Treasury bonds. Looking at these together, you'll find that what emerged in the US wasn't just a "stablecoin race," but a complete on-chain financial stack. Stablecoins act as on-chain cash, tokenized funds and treasuries as on-chain assets, market infrastructure handles registration, circulation, and custody, and payment, collateralization, clearing, and distribution grow on top of that. This is why I think that if our analysis of Hong Kong stablecoins only focuses on "why the first batch of licenses were issued to banks," our perspective is too narrow. What Hong Kong is truly vying for today is not just stablecoin payments, but rather the local currency interface, settlement gateway, and clearing position in the era of on-chain finance. Stablecoins are just the first brick. The real structure will not be merely a payment channel. III. The True Future of Hong Kong Dollar Stablecoins Lies in the Ecosystem. Of course, pessimists will naturally focus on the negative aspects. They might feel that giving the first batch to banks precisely demonstrates that Hong Kong has stifled innovation. Those best suited to maintain the old system may not be the most motivated to open up a new one. This concern is not unfounded. Mature institutions often possess neither technological nor compliance capabilities; what they truly lack is the willingness to rewrite their value chain. They are certainly willing to absorb new technologies into the old system, but they may not be willing to proactively promote a new path that could dilute their existing advantages. However, this pessimistic argument is only half true. The other half is that on-chain finance doesn't grow out of a vacuum. Without a trustworthy local currency settlement layer, without a compliant redemption mechanism, and without an entity capable of handling large-scale transactions and regulatory responsibilities, the subsequent payment, RWA, and tokenization ecosystems can only remain at the demo and fundraising levels. The most valuable lesson to learn from the US today isn't that "startups beat banks first," but rather the redistribution of labor and cooperation among traditional financial institutions, payment infrastructure companies, and native crypto companies within the same ecosystem. Hong Kong's decision to hand over the first key to banks may not be the end of innovation, but more likely just laying the foundation for the most difficult stage. Precisely because of this, I believe that what's truly worth watching after the initial license issuance isn't who holds the license, but who can develop capabilities beyond those licenses. Anchorpoint's proactive move towards a B2B2C model demonstrates that the issuer understands that having a license does not equate to having users. OSL's official statement further emphasizes its ecosystem, welcoming the initial license issuance and positioning itself in distribution, liquidity, and infrastructure partnerships. This signal is significant. It indicates that the initial concentration of issuance rights does not guarantee a similar concentration of long-term value. The real differentiator in the future may not be the issuance side, but rather the distribution side, wallets, payment interfaces, trading platforms, custody, compliance middleware, and who can connect on-chain cash to real assets and real transactions. Ultimately, the hardest part about stablecoins has never been issuing them, but retaining them. Payment is instantaneous, but financial matters take a whole night. Whether a company is willing to use Hong Kong dollar stablecoins for a settlement today only indicates that it has channel value; whether a company is willing to leave a portion of its operating funds in Hong Kong dollar stablecoins overnight tomorrow indicates that it begins to have balance value. Once settlements begin to convert into retention, stablecoins will transform from payment instruments into financial infrastructure. Taking it a step further, only when this portion of on-chain cash begins to naturally flow into tokenized funds, tokenized bonds, RWA trading, and collateral management will Hong Kong's stablecoin ecosystem truly connect with the entire on-chain financial ecosystem. This is also the point I'm most concerned about when I saw the first batch of licenses issued in Hong Kong today. If Hong Kong dollar stablecoins are only understood as a faster payment method, their potential is not very high. Hong Kong's local retail market is not large, and its payment infrastructure is already quite mature; relying solely on the front-end payment experience cannot tell a sufficiently compelling story. However, if it's placed within a larger structure—as an on-chain settlement layer for local currency, connecting payments, treasury, RWA, tokenization, and cross-border capital flows—its significance changes entirely. It's no longer just a Web3 product, but part of Hong Kong's digital financial infrastructure. Therefore, after this first batch of licenses was issued, my judgment is actually clearer than before. Hong Kong's first batch of stablecoin licenses were indeed given to the most conservative parties first. This is typical of Hong Kong, and it makes sense. However, the true potential of a Hong Kong dollar stablecoin won't be determined by the initial batch of listings, but rather by its ability to develop into a local settlement layer within Hong Kong's on-chain financial ecosystem. Banks can open the door first; what truly determines the market's viability is whether it can later develop distribution channels, interfaces, assets, and usage frequency. The first step has finally been taken. The real competition is only just beginning.

data.title: Everyone's talking about oil, but what the world really needs might be tokens.
data.description:
Author: Bao Yilong, Wall Street Insights

Computing power shortage is becoming the most intractable bottleneck in the AI ​​boom era.

Over the past few months, with the explosive growth in demand for "intelligent agents" (AI), the supply of computing resources has lagged far behind the rate of consumption. From frequent outages to soaring prices, cracks are spreading in the most vulnerable link of this technological boom.

Los Angeles engineer and technology investor Ben Pouladian said:

Everyone is talking about oil, but I think what the world is really short of is tokens.

Recently, Anthropic's popular programming tool, Claude Code, has experienced frequent service outages. The company has begun limiting user token usage during peak weekday hours, but this has resulted in numerous user complaints. Meanwhile, OpenAI has chosen to shut down its video generation application, Sora, to free up computing power to support its next-generation AI models. Cloud computing provider CoreWeave has raised its service prices by over 20% and is requiring smaller customers to sign long-term contracts of at least three years. The impact of this computing power crisis on the AI ​​industry is already clearly visible. Just as millions of enterprise users are deeply embedding AI tools into their workflows, the reliability and availability of these tools are declining simultaneously. The resulting loss of trust may become an unbearable burden for leading AI companies in the fierce competition for users. Tokens, a New Scarce Resource Tokens are the basic unit for measuring computing power consumption in the AI ​​field, directly corresponding to the computational overhead behind each model inference. On OpenAI's API platform, token calls surged from 6 billion per minute in October last year to 15 billion per minute at the end of March this year, an increase of 150% in less than six months. The core driving force behind this growth is the rapid popularization of "agent" AI. These tools can autonomously perform tasks, from writing software code to arranging property viewings for real estate agents, profoundly replacing workflows previously done manually. Ben Pouladian points out: AI is no longer just a chatbot that lets us ask for a recipe while standing in front of the refrigerator; it's orchestrating tasks, it's becoming smarter. Outages and rate limiting hit Anthropic hardest. Since mid-February this year, Anthropic's service outages have become increasingly frequent, and some enterprise customers have begun migrating workloads to other AI providers. As of April 8th, Anthropic's Claude API had an uptime of only 98.95% over the past 90 days. This figure falls far short of industry standards, which typically require software service providers to commit to 99.99% availability. Amir Haghighat, co-founder and CTO of AI inference startup Baseten, bluntly stated: AWS, databases, and payment platforms all require extremely high availability. However, the reality in the AI ​​field is not like that, and this falls far short of the quality of service you would expect from a company that provides intelligent capabilities for your applications. David Hsu, founder and CEO of software development platform Retool, said that he initially preferred using Anthropic's Opus 4.6 model to drive the company's AI agent tools, believing it performed best in enterprise scenarios, but recently switched to OpenAI's model. He said: Anthropic has been constantly down. Facing pressure, Anthropic announced at the end of March that it would limit token usage during peak hours from 5 a.m. to 11 a.m. Pacific Time on weekdays. This move quickly sparked a wave of complaints from users on social media. One user wrote on the X platform: "I haven't reached the Claude Code limit for several weeks, but this week I reached the limit in about 45 minutes." Ironically, the frequent outages occurred against the backdrop of Anthropic's rapid business growth. The company's annualized revenue jumped from $9 billion at the end of 2025 to $14 billion in just two months, and then doubled again to $30 billion. Prices Soar, GPU Rentals Surge 48% in Two Months Supply shortages have simultaneously driven up computing power costs. According to the computing power price index released by New York-based data service provider Ornn, the hourly cost of renting Nvidia's latest generation Blackwell chips is currently $4.08, a 48% increase from $2.75 two months ago. Vultr CEO J.J. Kardwell described the current situation as "a massive computing power shortage unseen in over five years." He explained: The problem is, why not just deploy more equipment? Because delivery cycles are too long, data center construction cycles are too long, and all available power capacity until 2026 has already been pre-booked. Bank of America analysts reinstated their coverage of CoreWeave at the end of last month, giving it a "buy" rating and predicting that demand for the company's services will continue to exceed supply until at least 2029. OpenAI CFO Sarah Friar also admitted in a recent public investor video interview: "I have indeed spent a lot of time trying to find any temporarily available computing power. We are currently making very difficult trade-offs on some things we wanted to move forward because of insufficient computing power." Historically, infrastructure can never catch up with demand. This dilemma is not unique to the AI ​​industry, but a classic proposition that runs through the history of technology. From the railway expansion of the 19th century to the telecommunications and internet bubble of the early 21st century, every technological boom has been accompanied by a lag in infrastructure development; demand growth has consistently outpaced resource acquisition and capacity expansion. Historical experience shows that price increases are often one of the few effective means of alleviating supply shortages. However, this option is particularly tricky for cutting-edge AI companies currently fiercely competing for users. Price increases could directly harm user growth, which is precisely the core pillar of their valuation logic. Finding a balance between maintaining service quality, controlling computing costs, and maintaining competitiveness will be the core challenge that the AI ​​industry must address in the next stage.

data.title: BTC targets $75,000; US-Iran negotiations reverse course again, could the market see a turnaround?
data.description:
Shaw, Jinse Finance

Starting in the early hours of April 14th, the crypto market rebounded strongly again. Bitcoin briefly broke through $74,000 this morning, reaching $74,904.2 at one point, a 24-hour increase of over 5%; Ethereum briefly broke through $2,300, reaching $2,394.50 at one point, a 24-hour increase of over 8.5%. Other major cryptocurrencies also rebounded to varying degrees.

Coinglass data shows that in the past 24 hours, a total of $531 million in positions were liquidated across the network, with $105 million in long positions and $426 million in short positions liquidated, mainly short positions. Among them, $229 million in Bitcoin short positions were liquidated and $136 million in Ethereum short positions were liquidated. In the past 24 hours, more than 177,200 people across the network have been liquidated.

Trump's statement that Iran remains willing to reach an agreement boosted investor risk appetite. US stocks closed higher, with the S&P 500 recovering all its year-to-date losses. US Treasury yields rose initially but then fell, and the dollar initially jumped due to safe-haven demand before declining throughout the day. Crude oil rose more than 9% at one point, but gains were limited by expectations of US-Iran peace talks. Currently, the news landscape is fraught with uncertainty, and the market remains focused on signals of diplomatic progress, stimulating a rise in risk assets. Global attention is now focused on the next steps in US-Iran negotiations. How will major asset markets react?

I. BTC Aims for $75,000, Crypto Market Rebounds Again, Risk Appetite Improves

Early this morning, the crypto market rebounded strongly again. Bitcoin surged rapidly this morning, briefly breaking through $74,000 and reaching $74,904.2, aiming for the $75,000 mark, with a 24-hour increase of over 5%. Ethereum also rebounded in tandem, briefly breaking through $2,300 and reaching $2,394.50, with a 24-hour increase of over 8.5%. Other major cryptocurrencies also rebounded to varying degrees.

According to Coinglass data, in the past 24 hours, a total of $531 million in positions were liquidated across the entire network, with $105 million in long positions and $426 million in short positions, primarily short positions. Specifically, $229 million in Bitcoin short positions were liquidated, and $136 million in Ethereum short positions were liquidated. Furthermore, in the past 24 hours, over 177,200 people across the network have been liquidated, with the largest single liquidation occurring in Aster - BTCUSDT, valued at $12.4072 million. Meanwhile, although US-Iran peace talks remained deadlocked, Trump indicated that Iran was still willing to reach an agreement, boosting investor risk appetite. US stock indices closed higher, with the S&P 500 recovering all its year-to-date losses. The S&P 500 rose 1.02%, the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.63%, and the Nasdaq Composite rose 1.23%. US Treasury yields rose initially but then fell, with the 10-year yield falling 1.98 basis points and the 2-year yield falling 1.89 basis points. The US dollar initially jumped due to safe-haven demand but subsequently declined throughout the day, ultimately closing at its lowest level since March 3. Crude oil initially rose more than 9%, but gains were limited by expectations of US-Iran peace talks, and it subsequently fell back below $100. Gold fell for the second consecutive day, with gold futures falling more than 3% at one point. The breakdown of US-Iran peace talks, coupled with Trump's announcement of a news blackout, triggered widespread risk aversion. However, according to Xinhua News Agency early this morning, Trump told the media at the White House that the US received a call that morning from "the right person" in Iran who expressed a desire to reach an agreement. US officials revealed that contact between the US delegation and Iranian leaders continues after the Islamabad talks, and "progress is currently being made in striving to reach an agreement." Although the news is full of uncertainty, the market still chooses to focus on signals of diplomatic progress, stimulating the rise of some risk assets. In the crypto market, the continued push for regulatory legislation and the continuous inflow of institutional buying funds have also increased liquidity, which is beneficial to the market rebound. II. US-Iran Talks Reverse Again and Again, Stimulating Market Risk Appetite Sources revealed that the US and Iran are discussing holding a new round of face-to-face negotiations to seek a longer-term ceasefire agreement. The source indicated that the goal is to hold a new round of talks before the two-week ceasefire agreement announced on April 7 expires next week. They mentioned that one option is to return to Islamabad for a second round of negotiations, but other locations are also under discussion. According to RIA Novosti, citing the American magazine *The Atlantic*, the next round of "direct negotiations" between the US and Iran may be held on the 16th in Islamabad, the capital of Pakistan. Previous negotiations, chaired by US Vice President Vance in Islamabad, failed to achieve a breakthrough. However, in an interview with Fox News, Vance stated that the US has made significant progress in negotiations with Iran. When asked if there would be more negotiations, Vance said the ball was in Tehran's court. He added that the US expects Iran to make progress on opening the Strait of Hormuz and warned that the situation would change if Tehran did not do so. Vance stated that the US delegation withdrew from negotiations after determining that Iranian negotiators lacked the authority to reach a final agreement, adding that any progress now depends on the approval of the Tehran leadership. Vance emphasized that Trump remains open to establishing a more normalized relationship with Iran, provided key conditions are met. However, he stated that for Iran to become an economically normal country, it must become a normal country that does not pursue nuclear weapons and also a normal country that does not engage in "terrorism." Iran, however, stated that the US's constantly changing demands during negotiations in Islamabad were the reason for the failure to reach an agreement. The Iranian Foreign Ministry stated on the 13th that Iranian Foreign Minister Araghchi, in a phone call with French Foreign Minister Barro on the evening of the 12th, said that despite Iran's "complete distrust" of the US, it participated in the negotiations in a responsible and goodwill manner and made progress on several issues. US President Trump hinted earlier this Monday that he was open to more dialogue, stating that Iran had proactively contacted the US. According to Xinhua News Agency, Trump told reporters at the White House on the 13th that Iran had called the US that morning, saying "they want to reach an agreement." However, Trump also stated that he would not accept any agreement that would allow Tehran to possess nuclear weapons. "Iran will not have nuclear weapons," Trump told reporters at the White House. "We cannot allow one country to blackmail or extort the world." The Iranian nuclear issue and uranium enrichment are among the focal points of the US-Iran standoff. According to AXIOS, a US official and a source familiar with the matter revealed that the US proposed a 20-year moratorium on uranium enrichment for Iran during negotiations in Islamabad over the weekend. Iran, however, proposed a shorter period, a single-digit number of years. Sources said the US demand for a moratorium on uranium enrichment was a key issue in these marathon negotiations. "The US proposed at least a 20-year moratorium, with various other restrictions attached," said a source familiar with the matter. The US also demanded that Iran remove all highly enriched uranium from the country. Iran stated that it was willing to accept a "monitored de-enrichment process" for these materials. Although the previous round of US-Iran negotiations did not yield significant results, recent statements from all parties indicate that the negotiations have progressed beyond expectations. The market is focusing on these positive developments, risk appetite has increased again, and this has further stimulated the rise of risk assets such as cryptocurrencies. III. The CLARITY Bill Progress Accelerates, Prospects Gradually Become Clearer On April 14, White House digital asset advisor Patrick Witt stated that the previously considered "intractable" differences in crypto legislation have significantly decreased, and the prospects for passing the relevant bill are improving. With Congress reconvening, the Senate's key committee is expected to vote on the CLARITY bill, paving the way for further legislation. This bill aims to clarify the regulatory division of labor between the SEC and CFTC and establish a unified regulatory framework for exchanges and market participants. The current focus of the controversy remains on stablecoin incentive mechanisms. While the passed stablecoin bill prohibits issuers from directly paying interest to users, disagreements persist regarding whether third-party platforms should be allowed to provide rewards. Overall, policy-making is shifting from "high uncertainty" to "gradual convergence," and the regulatory framework for the crypto industry is expected to enter a phase of substantial progress. Furthermore, according to Hodler's Digest, Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong publicly stated that after months of legislative delays and multi-party negotiations, the time is "right" for the passage of the US CLARITY Act. This statement represents a significant reversal of his stance on the bill. In January 2026, he twice publicly withdrew his support for the CLARITY Act, citing flaws in its provisions, directly causing the Senate Banking Committee to postpone its review and voting process, bringing the legislative process to a standstill. After months of bipartisan negotiations and multi-party consultations between the crypto industry and the banking sector, Armstrong stated that the current version of the bill has become a "powerful bill" and explicitly called on Congress to push for its enactment. As the most important comprehensive regulatory bill for the crypto market to date, the CLARITY Act is gradually becoming clearer after a long period of multi-party negotiations. The accelerated progress of this bill is one of the major positive factors currently supporting market trends. IV. Listed Treasury Companies Buy in Large Quantities; Institutional Buying Supports the Market. Bitcoin treasury company Strategy purchased 13,927 Bitcoins last week, totaling approximately $1 billion, at a price of approximately $71,902 per coin, achieving a 5.6% Bitcoin return year-to-date (2026). As of April 12, 2026, Strategy held 780,897 Bitcoins, with a total value of approximately $59.02 billion and an average price of approximately $75,577 per Bitcoin. As of April 12, Eastern Time, the total cryptocurrency, cash, and "Moonshot" holdings of Ethereum treasury company BitMine amounted to $11.8 billion. BitMine held 4,874,858 ETH (an increase of 71,524 ETH from the previous week), representing 4.04% of the total Ethereum supply (120.7 million ETH). It also held 197 BTC, $200 million worth of shares in Beast Industries, $85 million worth of shares in Eightco Holdings (NASDAQ: ORBS), and $719 million in uncollateralized cash. As of April 13, 2026, Bitmine had a total of 3,334,637 ETH staked (equivalent to $7.4 billion at $2,206 per ETH). Listed treasury companies, as major institutional buyers, have consistently purchased large amounts of related crypto assets, which is a significant factor supporting the market. When the market is sluggish, institutional buying can alleviate downward pressure. And when the market rebounds strongly, institutional buying becomes a major boost to asset prices. V. Continued Inflow of Funds into ETFs Improves Liquidity CoinShares monitoring data shows that last week, funds flowing into digital asset investment products reached $1.1 billion, the highest weekly inflow since early January. Bitcoin inflows totaled $871 million, bringing the total inflows so far this year to nearly $2 billion. Ethereum market sentiment rebounded strongly, with inflows reaching $196.5 million. XRP saw inflows of $19.3 million, while other assets experienced almost no inflows. Solana saw a small outflow of $2.5 million. Furthermore, US spot ETFs contributed 95% of the inflows. SoSoValue data shows that the US spot Bitcoin ETF saw a net inflow of $786 million last week, and the US spot Ethereum ETF saw a net inflow of $187 million. This continued improvement in ETF inflows is injecting liquidity into the crypto market, expanding market demand for cryptocurrencies, and thus driving the asset's upward trend.

VI. Market Analysis and Interpretation

1. Louis Miller, Head of Global Customized Equity Basket Business at Goldman Sachs, pointed out that current momentum indicators have not yet entered the overbought zone. As long as the fundamentals remain solid during the upcoming earnings season, this AI-driven breakout rally still has ample room to continue, especially in the AI ​​infrastructure sector. Market consensus estimates that the EPS growth rate of information technology stocks in the first quarter of this year will reach as high as 44%, which is undoubtedly the most crucial catalyst for this quarter. Despite the recent sharp fluctuations in gold prices, Goldman Sachs maintains its baseline forecast that gold prices will reach $5,400/ounce by the end of this year. The "three driving forces" supporting this aggressive target are: the continued diversification needs of global central banks, the gradual normalization of speculative positions, and the Federal Reserve's expected 50 basis point rate cut this year. Against this macroeconomic backdrop, gold mining companies have in fact benefited from positive operating leverage, significantly improved free cash flow, and have the potential to increase dividends.

8. Foreign exchange analyst Carter Johnson stated that in the market environment following the release of the CPI data, the US dollar spot index has cumulatively fallen by about 1.4%, and is expected to record its largest weekly decline since January. Regarding the clear macro trading strategy during the US-Iran conflict, buying the dollar and oil has been quite effective. However, as the foreign exchange market enters a phase where trading relies heavily on news headlines (this time, reports on negotiations against the backdrop of a fragile ceasefire), this strategy will face a test.

data.title: Why did AI agents emerge so suddenly, and why are they irreversible?
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

I. AI Becomes "Agentic Users," Defining New Boundaries for Human-Machine Collaboration

Recently, Microsoft previewed a new type of AI agent called "Agentic Users" in its product roadmap. These agents will have their own email accounts and can autonomously participate in meetings and handle tasks.This signifies that AI is evolving from a passive tool into an active collaborator with a certain "agent" identity.This transformation is not an isolated event, but rather the inevitable result of the long-term investment of tech giants like Microsoft in the field of AI Agents. Microsoft defines AI Agents as intelligent systems capable of automating repetitive, low-error-rate tasks by writing and executing code, thereby unlocking value in scenarios requiring massive data processing and precise computation, such as finance and education. However, as AI Agents become increasingly autonomous and even begin to mimic the "identity" of human employees, a series of fundamental questions arise: In cutting-edge fields such as quantum networks and digital finance, how will highly autonomous AI impact existing workflows and decision-making mechanisms? Does the "Rotifer Intelligent Agent Autonomous Evolution Protocol" represent a technological concept that foreshadows AI evolving independently, deviating from a predetermined path? Given the current imperfections in digital governance and compliance frameworks, how should we construct rules to ensure the prosperity of the open-source technology ecosystem while mitigating the risk of loss of control? These questions all point to a core issue: we are standing at a critical juncture in the paradigm shift of human-machine relationships, urgently needing to draw a clear blueprint for the upcoming "intelligent agent society." II. The Evolution from Automated Scripts to "Agent Users" The concept of AI Agents did not emerge overnight; its development has closely followed the leap in artificial intelligence capabilities over the past decade, particularly in Large Language Models (LLM). Microsoft research indicates that, by leveraging its ability to extract logical reasoning from data, large language models can support complex decision-making processes, helping to autonomously execute tasks and thus function as intelligent agents in various workflows. This technological foundation has enabled AI to evolve from executing simple, fixed automated scripts (such as traditional RPA robotic process automation) to "intelligent agents" capable of understanding natural language instructions and planning and executing multi-step tasks. Looking back at Microsoft's practical path, this evolutionary trajectory is clearly visible. Early on, AI applications focused on improving efficiency in specific scenarios. For example, in the medical field, intelligent Power Automate RPA processes were used to connect with hospital information systems (HIS), replacing large-scale repetitive administrative work and improving the resource utilization efficiency of medical teams. This can be seen as the prototype of an AI agent—focused on automating specific tasks. As the technology matured, the focus shifted to building more general and autonomous agent frameworks. Microsoft offers open-source tools and SDKs such as AutoGen and Semantic Kernel at the Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) level, aiming to provide enterprises with readily available and stable solutions for developing intelligent agents. The pinnacle of development lies in the exploration of "embodied intelligence" and universal agents. Microsoft's research team published a forward-looking paper on "Agent AI," the first attempt to pre-train a foundational model for developing general AI agents by integrating embodied data collected from fields such as robotics. From tools for improving efficiency to programmable frameworks, and then to "agent users" pursuing versatility and autonomy, AI agents have undergone a transformation from mere "technique" to "principle" over the past decade, laying the historical and technological foundation for today's widespread applications. III. Technological Breakthroughs, Business Needs, and Ecosystem Competition Jointly Drive the Agent Wave Why has the AI ​​Agent suddenly emerged and become the focus of the industry at this particular moment? Behind it lies the intertwining and resonance of three driving forces: technology, demand, and ecosystem. First, continuous breakthroughs in core technologies are the fundamental driving force. The leaps in large language models in code generation (such as WaveCoder), logical reasoning, and contextual understanding have given AI Agents a "brain." Cloud computing platforms provide powerful computing power and a stable operating environment, while open-source frameworks have significantly lowered the development threshold. For example, Microsoft, through tools such as the Semantic Kernel, allows developers to more easily build intelligent agents that understand semantics and call external tools and APIs. These technological advancements collectively address the key questions of whether intelligent agents "can think" and "how to act." Secondly, the urgent needs of enterprises for cost reduction, efficiency improvement, and digital transformation provide market pull. In an increasingly competitive global market, companies are eager to free employees from repetitive, low-value labor, allowing them to focus on innovation and strategic decision-making. AI Agents excel at this, processing massive amounts of data and performing precise calculations with "high efficiency and low error rates." From risk modeling in the financial industry to process optimization in manufacturing, intelligent agents have become the core engine for enterprises to unleash the potential of data and build intelligent applications. Industry events such as Microsoft AI Summit Taipei, with AI Agents as their central theme, reflect the strong expectation of the business community for a new chapter in human-machine collaboration. Finally, strategic positioning for building the future ecosystem creates competitive momentum. AI Agents are considered the core entry point and operating system for next-generation human-computer interaction. Whoever controls the dominant platform and protocols for intelligent agents is likely to occupy a pivotal position in the future digital ecosystem. Microsoft is vigorously promoting its Copilot and Agent ecosystem and continuously holding the "Microsoft AI Genius" series of developer events, aiming to consolidate its full-stack advantages from development tools to cloud platforms, gather a developer community, and build a thriving ecosystem of intelligent agent applications. This platform-level competition accelerates the process of AI Agent technology moving from the laboratory to industrial applications. IV. Constructing a Three-in-One Intelligent Agent Development System: Framework-Evolution-Governance Faced with the opportunities and challenges brought by AI Agents, we need a systematic solution, not just piecemeal technical fixes. This system should encompass three levels: technical framework, evolution mechanism, and governance rules. First, rely on robust open-source frameworks to lower the application threshold and ensure security and controllability. Enterprises introducing AI Agents should not reinvent the wheel but should base them on proven open-source frameworks. Tools like Microsoft's AutoGen and Semantic Kernel, supported by official teams, provide readily available and stable solutions. These define standard ways for intelligent agents to interact with the external world (such as through the MCP-Model Context Protocol), but we must also acknowledge the current protocols' security shortcomings and actively improve them through community contributions. Enterprises can leverage this foundation, combining their expertise in digital finance, quantum network simulation, and other fields, to develop intelligent agents for vertical scenarios, achieving rapid and secure deployment. Secondly, we should explore controlled autonomous evolution protocols to guide the positive growth of intelligent agent capabilities. Concepts like the "Rotifer intelligent agent autonomous evolution protocol" represent a cutting-edge direction for enabling AI to learn and iteratively optimize itself in specific environments. The key is "controlled." In highly simulated digital twin environments (such as virtual financial markets or quantum computing networks), we can set clear evolutionary goals and boundary rules for intelligent agents, allowing them to autonomously explore strategies through reinforcement learning and other methods. This not only accelerates the growth of AI's application capabilities in complex fields but also confines the evolutionary process within a secure sandbox, providing valuable data for studying its behavioral patterns. Third, establish a forward-looking digital governance and compliance framework to set rules for an intelligent agent society. When AI agents become "proxy users," existing legal and ethical frameworks face direct challenges. Solutions must be developed first. This includes: defining the legal responsibility of intelligent agents (developers, users, or the intelligent agent itself?); establishing auditing and traceability mechanisms for their operations to ensure transparency in decision-making in key areas such as financial transactions; and developing data privacy and security standards to prevent intelligent agents from abusing their authority. The construction of a governance framework requires the joint participation of technical experts, legal scholars, policymakers, and business representatives, and should be integrated into the design of the open-source technology ecosystem to achieve "governance as code." V. AI Agents Are Irreversible, Require Safety, Inclusivity, and Benevolence. The allure of AI agents is irreversible. While actively developing them, we must remain clear-headed and avoid several potential pitfalls and risks. First, be wary of the illusion of "complete autonomy" and adhere to the fundamental principle of human-in-the-loop. No matter how intelligent an AI agent is, its essence remains an extension of human intent and design. The "agent user" described by Microsoft still aims to improve the efficiency of "human-machine collaboration." We must avoid designing or using "strongly autonomous intelligent agents" that are completely free from human supervision and can set their own ultimate goals. Key decisions, especially in areas such as medical diagnosis, financial risk control, and judicial assessment, must retain the final review and veto power of human experts. The technical architecture should include built-in "circuit breakers" and intervention channels. Secondly, it is crucial to guard against the risks of widening the digital divide and ecosystem lock-in. Powerful AI agent platforms and frameworks may be dominated by a few tech giants, potentially preventing SMEs from equally enjoying technological benefits due to excessively high technical and financial barriers, thus exacerbating the digital divide. Simultaneously, over-reliance on closed ecosystems of single vendors carries the risk of lock-in. Therefore, while embracing excellent solutions provided by companies like Microsoft, the industry should actively promote the development of cross-platform interoperability standards and encourage the development of a diverse and open open-source technology ecosystem to ensure a healthy competitive and innovative environment. Thirdly, attention should be paid to the challenges of employment structure transformation and social adaptation. While AI agents automate a large number of tasks, they inevitably impact existing jobs. Society cannot only focus on technology deployment but also needs to simultaneously plan for workforce retraining and education system reform. Future education should emphasize cultivating creativity, critical thinking, and the ability to work collaboratively with AI to help workers adapt to the new human-machine symbiotic work model. Enterprises also need to take responsibility for providing transition paths for affected employees. Fourth, ethical and bias issues will amplify with increased autonomy, requiring continuous governance. Intelligent agents, based on data training and interactive learning, may inherit or even amplify existing biases and injustices in human society. This harm will be amplified when they are given more autonomous decision-making power. Therefore, ethical review and bias detection of AI agents must be carried out throughout their entire lifecycle of development, deployment, and evolution, becoming a continuous governance effort rather than a one-time certification. Looking to the future, the evolution of AI agents is irreversible, ushering in a new chapter of intelligent applications. The success of this revolution depends not only on the elegance of the code and the power of the algorithms, but also on our ability to build a safe, inclusive, and benevolent development framework for it with a high sense of responsibility and forward-thinking wisdom. Only in this way can intelligent agents truly become powerful partners for humanity in expanding the boundaries of cognition and solving complex challenges, jointly moving towards a more efficient and creative future.

data.title: The US blockade may completely rewrite the situation.
data.description:
Author: Anthony Pompliano, Founder and CEO of Professional Capital Management; Translated by Shaw Jinse Finance

In the past few days, the United States and Iran were preparing to consolidate the ceasefire achieved through a peace agreement reached in Pakistan. After 21 hours of negotiations, US Vice President JD Vance took to the stage to announce to the world that the peace agreement would not be signed.

According to reports, Iran still refused to accept the US proposal, and the fighting has therefore restarted. This time, however, the situation has taken a major turn.

The United States did not allow Iran to block the Strait of Hormuz, but instead decided to proactively block it. At first glance, this decision seems like a naive move to insist on winning in a small conflict, but a deeper analysis reveals that this move has significant strategic implications.

For example, in the past few weeks, Iran has not completely blocked the Strait of Hormuz, but rather prevented most ships from passing through, only imposing taxes on some. This selective control has brought huge fiscal revenues to the Middle Eastern country, especially since Iran also exports its oil through the strait during the same period. A US blockade would quickly cut off all of Iran's economic gains, preventing it from controlling the strait and profiting from it. Miad Maleki, a senior researcher at the FDD Foundation, wrote: "A US Navy blockade of the Strait of Hormuz would cause Iran to lose approximately $276 million in exports and $159 million in imports daily, totaling approximately $435 million in economic losses per day, amounting to as much as $13 billion per month." Such a scale of economic loss would exert maximum pressure on Iran and also exacerbate the economic pressure on Iran's allies. Iran's decision to weaponize the Strait of Hormuz is illogical, as it cannot deliver on its threats as expected. Zineb Riboua of the Hudson Institute wrote: "Iran's weaponization of the Strait of Hormuz is one of its most strategically reckless decisions. The country lacks the capacity for long-term control of the strait, and its military posture cannot be sustained against a determined adversary. More importantly, it has never been a credible bargaining chip. Once the situation escalates, control will ultimately be relinquished without substantial concessions. The only real bargaining chip has always been its nuclear program. The logic behind this decision is also flawed. Iran hoped to trigger a massive global economic shock, forcing the United States to halt military action, but this did not happen. Instead, the situation has accelerated in the opposite direction. Regional and global parties are working to build shipping routes and infrastructure that completely bypass the strait." Iran's attempt to use the Strait of Hormuz as a bargaining chip is actually undermining its long-term strategic value. This move by the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps is extremely foolish. Other reports can be interpreted regarding the geopolitical implications of this decision; therefore, this article will focus on analyzing how this development will affect the US economy and its people. First, in terms of oil exports, the US has become the most sought-after partner globally. Hundreds of oil tankers worldwide are heading to the Gulf of Mexico, vying to buy US oil. Whether this is a deliberate outcome or not, US energy independence is rapidly becoming a strategic asset, not only driving significant revenue growth in its domestic oil and gas industry but also benefiting the entire nation.

Secondly, the US may incur short-term military and operational costs, including mine clearance, naval escort, and expenses related to a potential conflict with Iranian forces. These additional expenditures will only further exacerbate national debt pressures and, in the long run, lead to a continued depreciation of the dollar.

Thirdly, oil prices are likely to continue to fluctuate wildly. Global markets are trying to determine a reasonable price per barrel of crude oil, but production costs, total global supply, and future production expectations are changing daily.

The market generally believes that a fully open Strait of Hormuz would depress oil prices, while a complete blockade would only push them higher. Fourth, the US stock market will experience volatility. Software stocks and the broader technology sector face significant pressure, while energy and oil stocks have consistently outperformed in recent weeks. These trends may reverse if the conflict ends, but the market is likely to remain volatile for the foreseeable future. Finally, the US domestic economy will present a contradictory situation. As I wrote last week, the US economy will continue to benefit from high oil prices, but ordinary people will bear the burden of rising oil prices. This will further reinforce expectations of high inflation, especially given rising fertilizer and food prices, as well as increasing global shipping costs. In the short term, the negative impact on American households is unavoidable. The key questions are: how long will this impact last? Will prices fall quickly after the war ends? If the stock market surges this summer, will the American public forget this episode? How will the midterm elections affect the geopolitical decisions the Trump administration is weighing? I don't yet have definitive answers to many of these questions. But it's clear that the US blockade will fundamentally change Iran's position in this conflict. Iran has gone from being in control to being globally isolated. Regardless of its public statements, no one can be indifferent to the $13 billion monthly loss. We can only hope that this escalation will lead to a genuine ceasefire. War is cruel, and no one wants to see innocent people die.

data.title: AI-Driven Unrestricted Financial Warfare: Alarms Raised by the Anthropic Mythos Model
data.description:
I. Event Overview: US Top Leaders Urgently Convene Meeting with Banking Giants to Address New AI Risks

In early April 2026, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell urgently convened a meeting at the Treasury Department headquarters in Washington, D.C., with the CEOs of several systemically important banks to discuss the cybersecurity risks posed by Anthropic's latest AI model, Mythos (Claude Mythos Preview). This closed-door meeting was hastily arranged around April 7th. Participants included Citigroup CEO Jane Fraser, Morgan Stanley CEO Ted Pick, Bank of America CEO Brian Moynihan, Wells Fargo CEO Charlie Scharf, and Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon. JP Morgan CEO Jamie Dimon was unable to attend due to other commitments.

Technology Defense

Banks and infrastructure operators should invest in AI-assisted defense tools, implement continuous vulnerability scanning, sandbox isolation, and human oversight. Zero-trust architecture and quantum-secure encryption will be key focuses.

Enterprise and Individual Level

Diversify asset allocation and avoid over-reliance on a single digital system. Physical assets such as precious metals can serve as long-term hedging tools, but should be integrated into the overall portfolio.

International Coordination AI risks know no borders. Major economies such as the US, China, and the EU need to engage in dialogue on security standards to prevent arms race-style proliferation. In the long term, AI will reshape the financial and security landscape. The Mythos incident is an early warning sign, indicating that accelerated technological development may amplify vulnerabilities. Balancing innovation and security is a common global challenge. VII. Conclusion: Building Resilience Amidst the Double-Edged Sword of Technology The emergency meeting triggered by the Anthropic Mythos model highlights the disruptive potential of AI in cybersecurity. It is both a powerful defensive tool and a potential offensive weapon. For the global financial system, this serves as a reminder of the vulnerability of digital dependence. In an era of heightened uncertainty, gold and silver, as "non-intelligent" assets, offer a path back to fundamental value. Future development depends on the efforts of multiple parties: technology companies need to strengthen security and ethics, regulators need to update frameworks, and market participants need to enhance resilience. Precious metals will not solve all problems, but in their physical stability and historical role, they embody humanity's pursuit of lasting value. Through careful analysis and diversified preparation, societies can better navigate technological progress while mitigating systemic risks.

data.title: The SEC clarifies regulations for cryptocurrency trading interfaces; the next round of US-Iran talks may be held on the 16th.
data.description:
Headline

▌US SEC Statement Clarifies Regulatory Boundaries for Crypto Trading Interfaces: Brokerage Licenses Exempt in Specific Scenarios

The US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) Division of Trading and Markets issued a staff statement providing guidance on whether certain covered user interfaces used to generate trading instructions for crypto asset securities need to be registered as brokers. The statement points out that, under certain conditions, providers of such interfaces may not need to register as broker-dealers under Section 15 of the Securities Exchange Act. These conditions include: not actively soliciting specific trades, not providing investment advice, not controlling or executing trades, generating trading instructions solely based on objective parameters, and fully disclosing to users the fee structure, potential conflicts of interest, and related risks. The SEC emphasizes that such interfaces typically exist in the form of websites, browser plugins, or wallet applications, used to convert user-defined trading parameters into on-chain executable instructions, while also providing market data such as prices, paths, and fees.

Such exemptions do not apply to activities such as participating in transaction matching, fund custody, order routing, or providing investment advice. These guidelines are interim opinions and will automatically expire five years later, starting April 13, 2026, unless further action is taken. This move aims to provide a clearer regulatory framework for activities related to crypto asset securities and to continuously solicit market feedback.

▌Next Round of US-Iran Talks May Be Held in Islamabad on the 16th

On April 14, according to a report by RIA Novosti citing the US magazine *The Atlantic*, the next round of "direct talks" between the United States and Iran may be held on the 16th in Islamabad, the capital of Pakistan.

(Xinhua News Agency) Market Data As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $74,758.68, a 24-hour change of +5.8%; ETH price is $2,376.19, a 24-hour change of +8.6%; BNB price is $616.52, a 24-hour change of +4.3%. SOL price is $86.50, a 24-hour change of +6.1%; DOGE price is $0.09414, a 24-hour change of +3.9%; XRP price is $1.38, a 24-hour change of +4.1%; TRX price is $0.321, a 24-hour change of... WLFI price is $0.08187, a 24-hour change of +4.6%; HYPE price is $44.28, a 24-hour change of +8.5%. Policy ▌White House advisor says crypto legislation disagreements are narrowing, progress is accelerating. On April 14, White House digital asset advisor Patrick Witt stated that previously considered "intractable" crypto legislation disagreements have significantly decreased, and the prospects for passing related legislation are improving. With Congress reconvening, a key Senate committee is expected to vote on a comprehensive crypto bill, paving the way for further legislation. This bill aims to clarify the regulatory roles of the SEC and CFTC and establish a unified regulatory framework for exchanges and market participants. Current controversy centers on stablecoin incentive mechanisms. While the passed stablecoin bill prohibits issuers from directly paying interest to users, disagreement remains regarding whether third-party platforms should be allowed to provide rewards. Overall, policy-making is shifting from "high uncertainty" to "gradual convergence," and the regulatory framework for the crypto industry is expected to enter a phase of substantial progress.

▌Coinbase CEO: The Time is Ripe for the US Clarity Act

April 13th news, according to Hodler's Digest, a subsidiary of Cointelegraph Magazine, Brian Armstrong, CEO of Coinbase, a leading US cryptocurrency exchange, publicly stated that after months of legislative delays and multi-party negotiations, the time is "ripe" for the passage of the US Clarity Act (the Digital Asset Markets Clarity Act). It is understood that Armstrong's statement represents a significant reversal of his stance on the bill. In January 2026, he twice publicly withdrew his support for the Clarity Act, citing flaws in its provisions, directly causing the Senate Banking Committee to postpone the bill's review and voting process, bringing the legislative process to a standstill.

... After months of bipartisan negotiations and extensive consultations between the crypto industry and the banking sector, Armstrong stated that the current version of the bill is a "powerful bill" and explicitly called on Congress to push for its enactment. The CLARITY Act, formally known as the Digital Asset Markets Clarity Act of 2025 (H.R. 3633), is the most comprehensive and core piece of cryptocurrency regulatory legislation ever enacted in the United States. Passed by a bipartisan majority in the U.S. House of Representatives on July 17, 2025, its core purpose is to clearly define the regulatory authority of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) over digital assets, clarify the classification standards for "digital commodities" and "digital asset securities," end the long-standing regulatory ambiguity in the U.S. crypto market, and provide a framework for the industry's compliant development.

▌Vance Says Significant Progress Made in Iran Talks; Second Round of Negotiations Depends on Iran

On April 14, US Vice President Vance stated in an interview with Fox News that the US had made significant progress in negotiations with Iran. When asked if there would be more negotiations, Vance said the ball was in Tehran's court. He added that the US expects Iran to make progress on opening the Strait of Hormuz and warned that if Tehran does not do so, the situation in the negotiations will change.

▌Iranian Foreign Ministry: US "Constant Changes of Mind" Led to Failure to Reach Agreement

On April 13, the Iranian Foreign Ministry stated that the US's constant changes of demands during negotiations with Iran in Islamabad were the reason why the two sides failed to reach an agreement.

The Iranian Foreign Ministry said that Iranian Foreign Minister Araqchi stated in a phone call with French Foreign Minister Barro on the evening of April 12 that although Iran had "complete distrust" of the United States, it still participated in the negotiations in a responsible and goodwill manner and made progress on several issues. However, due to excessive demands and constant changes in the US's demands, an agreement was ultimately not reached. (Xinhua)

Blockchain Applications

According to market news: MetaPlanet's American Depositary Receipts (ADR, ticker symbol MPJPY) are now tradable on Robinhood; its depository bank, Deutsche Bank, announced that it will waive the ADR issuance fee until June 12, reducing the entry cost for US retail investors.

▌Polka Cross-Chain Bridge Attacked, 1 Billion DOT Tokens Maliciously Minted and Sold Off

On April 13, according to PeckShield monitoring, 1 billion DOT tokens were abnormally minted and sold off on the Ethereum chain.

On-chain data shows that about an hour ago, attackers tampered with administrator privileges, transferring control of the contract to a malicious address, then immediately issued 1 billion DOT tokens and sold them off, causing the price to plummet from $1.22 to near zero. The incident is still developing, and Polkadot has not yet issued a response. The target of this attack was a bridge asset on Ethereum, not the Polkadot native chain.

Important Economic Developments

▌US Military Begins Intercepting Ships Entering and Exiting the Strait of Hormuz

On the 13th local time, the US military began intercepting ships entering and exiting the Strait of Hormuz. The US Central Command said on the 12th that, according to the president's order, it would begin imposing a blockade on all maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports at 10:00 AM Eastern Time on the 13th. The statement said that the blockade applies to all ships of all countries entering and exiting Iranian ports and the country's coastal areas, covering all Iranian ports in the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman.

▌US Media: US Demands Iran Suspend Uranium Enrichment for 20 Years; Iran Hopes for Shorter Suspension Period

On April 14, according to AXIOS, a US official and a source familiar with the matter revealed that the US proposed a 20-year suspension of uranium enrichment for Iran during negotiations held in Islamabad over the weekend. Iran, however, proposed a shorter period, a single-digit number of years. Sources said the US demand for a suspension of uranium enrichment was a key issue in this marathon negotiation. "The US proposed at least a 20-year suspension, with various other restrictions attached," said a source familiar with the matter. The US also demanded that Iran remove all highly enriched uranium from the country. Iran, on the other hand, stated that they were willing to accept a "monitored de-enrichment process" for these materials.

...

▌Trump: Will Not Accept Any Agreement Allowing Iran to Possess Nuclear Weapons

On April 14, local time, US President Trump stated on Monday that Iran wants an agreement, but he will not accept any agreement that would allow Tehran to possess nuclear weapons. Trump said that negotiations surrounding the nuclear issue have reached a stalemate and indicated that a “blockade” against ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz has begun. He stated that Iran “called this morning” and said that “they want to reach an agreement.”

... "Iran will not have nuclear weapons," Trump told reporters at the White House. "We cannot allow one country to blackmail or extort the world." ▌Federal Reserve's Goolsby: Strong US Consumer Performance Key to the US Economy On April 14, Federal Reserve's Goolsby stated on Monday that current pricing in the oil futures market reflects an expectation that the surge in oil prices related to the war with Iran will be short-lived, and its impact on the US economy is likely to be limited as long as this holds true. In an interview with Fox News, Goolsby stated, "As long as consumers remain strong, I think economic growth will remain strong. If that continues—if gasoline prices remain at or above their current levels for an extended period—then we'll have to reassess what that means for consumer spending; but if things move as the market expects, then it will be temporary." Vance reveals details of negotiations: Impasse stems from insufficient Iranian authorization. On April 14th, according to Fox News, US Vice President Vance stated that the US delegation withdrew from negotiations after determining that Iranian negotiators lacked the authorization to reach a final agreement, adding that any progress now depends on approval from the Tehran leadership. He said, "I think we really understand some of the Iranians' negotiating style, and that's ultimately why we left Pakistan, because we found that, I think, the team there couldn't reach an agreement, and they had to go back to Tehran, either to get approval from the Supreme Leader or someone else, to accept the conditions we offered." Vance emphasized that Trump remains open to a more normalized relationship with Iran, provided key conditions are met. However, he stated that for Iran to become an economically normal country, it must become a normal country that does not pursue nuclear weapons, and it must also become a normal country that does not engage in "terrorism."

▌Vance: No Agreement Reached, But Progress is Smooth in Some Areas

On April 14, according to Fox News, US Vice President Vance stated that although no agreement was reached, progress was "very smooth" in some areas.

He said, "I wouldn't say things went completely wrong. I think there were good parts." He added, "(We clarified) under which terms the U.S. could compromise, on which terms we had flexibility, and what outcomes we absolutely needed to see in order for President Trump to feel he had gotten a good deal. In short, we had some good conversations. I think this is probably the first time the Iranian and U.S. governments have held such a high-level meeting since the current Iranian leadership came to power." According to CME's "FedWatch" on April 14th, the probability of the Fed raising interest rates by 25 basis points in April is 1%, and the probability of keeping rates unchanged is 99%. The probability of the Fed cumulatively cutting interest rates by 25 basis points by June is 1.5%, the probability of keeping rates unchanged is 97.5%, and the probability of cumulatively raising interest rates by 25 basis points is 1%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" During Geopolitical Crises?

Every geopolitical crisis causes gold prices to rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been supported by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation).

Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," which can indeed function when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries are reduced, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold but rather a completely new "next-generation gold."

Generational shifts and the widespread application of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

A paper published Thursday by the researchers measured malicious man-in-the-middle attacks in the LLM supply chain, revealing four attack vectors, including malicious code injection and credential extraction. Co-author Chaofan Shou stated on X, "There are 26 LLM routers that are secretly injecting malicious tool calls and stealing credentials." Currently, LLM agents are increasingly forwarding requests through third-party API intermediaries or routers that aggregate access to service providers such as OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google. However, these routers terminate the Internet's TLS (Transport Layer Security) connection, thus enabling access to all message content in plaintext.

... This means that developers using AI coding agents (such as Claude Code) to develop smart contracts or wallets may unknowingly transfer their private keys. **DeFi Hotspot** **1. A trader made a profit of $150,000 by buying DOT on-chain at a cost of $600.** **On April 13th, according to on-chain analyst Ai Yi, a trader spent 0.2 ETH to buy 77,000 DOT after the Hyperbridge attackers dumped 1 billion DOT on the Ethereum mainnet, and then sold them through the OKX platform for $10,042.** Using the same method, they successively spent 1.1 ETH to buy DOT, bought low and sold high in different liquidity pools, and crossed them to the Base network via Hyperbridge. They then sold 503,000 DOT for 68.72 ETH, making a profit of $150,000 on just over $400, achieving a return of over 200 times.
