data.title: Thirty-Year-Old Code: The Battle for AI Payment Standards
data.description:
Author: Hazel Hu Source: X, @withhazelhu

Recently, AI payment has seen a surge in popularity, making a protocol standards war surrounding "how AI agents can spend money autonomously" inevitable.

In 1996, when Tim Berners-Lee and his team at CERN finalized the HTTP/1.0 specification, they left a blank space in the status code list: 402 Payment Required.

This was an interface reserved for future digital cash or micro-payment solutions. The creators of the internet foresaw thirty years ago that a toll lane would eventually be needed on the information superhighway.

... But the 402 error was never activated. No browser supported it, and no server actually used it. Like a letter with a proper address but never sent, it slumbered in the corner of the HTTP protocol for thirty years. Until AI Agent arrived. In the third week of March 2026, the payment industry saw four major events within 72 hours. On March 17, Mastercard acquired stablecoin infrastructure company BVNK for $1.8 billion. On March 18, Tempo, a public blockchain incubated by Stripe and Paradigm, officially launched its mainnet and simultaneously released the Machine Payments Protocol (MPP). On the same day, Visa Crypto Labs released visa-cli, a command-line tool that allows AI Agents to swipe cards directly on the terminal; Visa's head of crypto, Cuy Sheffield, named it "Command-Line Commerce." Prior to this, the x402 protocol, jointly launched by Coinbase and Cloudflare, had been running for nearly a year. Four pieces, one table. A long-brewing battle for standards has officially begun. The story begins with 402. Marc Andreessen once called the internet's lack of native payment capabilities "the original sin of the internet." The HTTP protocol is inherently capable of transmitting text, images, and videos, but not money. This deficiency gave rise to the entire advertising economy; since users are unwilling to pay for content, advertisers will foot the bill. Thus, we have a free but monitored internet. 402 could have changed all of this. Imagine this: your browser accesses a paid article, the server returns a 402 status code, the browser pops up a built-in micropayment interface, you click confirm, a penny is credited to your account, and the article is unlocked. The whole process is as natural as loading an image. However, the technology of the 1990s couldn't realize this vision. There were no mature solutions for digital cash, no low-cost settlement channels, and SSL and TLS were just beginning. The 402 became a placeholder ahead of its time. Thirty years later, it was awakened by a Canadian. Erik Reppel, a graduate of the University of Victoria's Computer Science department, was the head of engineering at the Web3 social company Zora before joining Coinbase. After joining Coinbase's developer platform, he began what he called "a project Coinbase has been exploring since 2015": designing a native payment standard for the internet. One of his inspirations came from the Bitcoin payment channel experiment conducted by Balaji Srinivasan, former CTO of Coinbase, during his time at 21.co. The idea behind that solution was sound, but it failed due to cost. Back then, the gas fee for each on-chain transaction was several dollars, making micropayments unworkable. However, by 2024, Layer 2 networks had reduced the cost of a single transaction to less than one ten-thousandth of a cent. The time had finally come. In May 2025, Reppel, along with colleagues Nemil Dalal and Dan Kim, jointly published a white paper, officially launching the x402 protocol. Its logic is clean and concise: a client requests a resource, the server returns an HTTP 402 response and a JSON payload specifying the price, accepted tokens, and wallet address; the client signs the payment with their wallet, settlement is completed on-chain, and the server returns the resource. The entire process takes two seconds. x402 chose the purest route: protocol-native, on-chain settlement, and no intermediaries. It's directly embedded in the HTTP layer, attempting to become part of the internet's infrastructure, like DNS and TLS. Coinbase and Cloudflare subsequently jointly established the x402 Foundation, entrusting protocol governance to a neutral foundation. What does Cloudflare's involvement mean? Approximately 20% of global network traffic passes through Cloudflare's more than 300 data centers, and x402 is integrated into its Agents SDK and MCP server. This is a distribution advantage that is difficult for any competing protocol to replicate. But another Canadian thinks differently. Liam Horne, a graduate of the University of Waterloo, entered the Ethereum field in 2016. He was classmates with Vitalik Buterin in school (they both received the Thiel Scholarship). His initial attraction to blockchain was simple: direct transfers between two pieces of software could occur without a bank. But Ethereum was too slow and too expensive. In 2017, Horne and Jeff Coleman proposed the concept of "generalized state channels" at L4 Ventures. The two parties lock their funds into a multi-signature contract, exchange signatures for transactions off-chain at any speed, and finally settle only the net result back on-chain. Zero fees, instant settlement, theoretically capable of thousands of transactions per second. They also invented a technique called "counterfactual instantiation," treating the contract as a deployed entity for interaction, even though it never actually touches the chain. This idea directly inspired Vitalik to propose EIP-1014, which later became the widely used CREATE2 opcode for account abstraction and cross-chain deployment. Horne's proudest demo is called Web3Torrent: a torrent download client where downloaders and uploaders pay small fees block-by-block through state channels. A tiny amount is paid for each downloaded file fragment. Machines pay machines, no human involvement. But the problem is—nobody needs this. From 2017 to 2020, the main theme of the Ethereum ecosystem was DeFi and transactions, not payments. State channels were technically feasible, but there were no users. It was like a tollbooth that had been built, but no cars on the highway. Horne admitted that state channels had become a "solution without problems." Rollups became a more general solution for scaling, and he turned to Optimism. At Optimism, Horne's resume rose rapidly: lead engineer, CEO, expanding the team from 8 to nearly 70, leading the development of the OP Stack, and driving the collaboration between Superchain and Base. Base is Coinbase's L2 chain. The two threads converged for the first time here. Later, Horne left OP Labs to become a consultant and also participated in the construction of World Chain. In the summer of 2024, he began intensive research on stablecoins. The success of Base made him realize one thing: the synergy between Coinbase, Circle, and Base almost replicated the stablecoin expansion path of Tether, Tron, and Binance outside the United States. Stablecoins are no longer a niche narrative; they account for 70% of all blockchain transaction volume. In September 2025, Tempo completed a $500 million Series A funding round at a $5 billion valuation, co-incubated by Stripe and Paradigm. Horne served as the head of engineering. Paradigm's CTO Georgios Konstantopoulos and co-founder Matt Huang were also deeply involved. Horne spent six years scaling Ethereum, first working on state channels, then Optimism. Then he left to work on a decentralized L1 architecture backed by a large corporation. In the same week that Tempo's mainnet launched, OP Labs announced a 20% layoff, and Vitalik had previously publicly rejected the L2 route. His classmate and fellow scholarship recipient seemed to have made his choice earlier than him. This wasn't the only intriguing instance of personnel movement. In February 2026, Dan Romero and Varun Srinivasan, co-founders of Farcaster, a decentralized social application strongly supported by Vitalik, announced they were joining Tempo. Before founding Farcaster, Romero was Coinbase's 20th employee, joining in 2014 and rising through the ranks to become VP, responsible for international and consumer business. Srinivasan also came from Coinbase, where he was responsible for engineering and product. Their departure from Farcaster was quite dramatic. After Neynar acquired the Farcaster protocol, the founding team collectively resigned, and Romero announced he would return all $180 million in funding to investors. He later wrote on X: "Stablecoins are a generation's opportunity." And that's not all. Tempo also poached Dankrad Feist, one of the key architects of the Ethereum L1 scaling roadmap, from the Ethereum Foundation. This means that Tempo's core team includes at least two former Coinbase executives, a former CEO of Ethereum L2, and a former Ethereum Foundation researcher. Meanwhile, Coinbase is pushing forward with x402. In a sense, this battle of standards is both a showdown between Coinbase alumni and a quiet split within the Ethereum ecosystem. So, what exactly does Tempo's MPP do? And how does it differ from x402? Horne's MPP protocol for Tempo can be summarized in one sentence: the Session mode of state channels has been moved to the application layer. In the world of x402, an agent completes an on-chain transaction every time it calls an API. This is fine in low-frequency scenarios, but when an AI agent needs to call the interface thousands of times within minutes, each on-chain transaction means significant friction and latency. MPP's solution is Sessions: the agent performs a handshake authentication, sets a spending limit, and then freely calls hundreds or thousands of APIs within this Session. All spending is packaged and settled at the end of the Session. Formally, this is identical to the state channels Horne implemented on Ethereum nine years ago: locked funds, off-chain interaction, and a single settlement. Another key difference is the payment method. x402 uses pure on-chain stablecoin settlement, inherently decentralized but also inherently excluding fiat currency. MPP, on the other hand, takes a "payment track-independent" approach, supporting stablecoins, credit cards, and even the Bitcoin Lightning Network. Lightning Network integration is handled by Lightspark, which itself is a Bitcoin version of "state channels." It's worth noting that Lightspark's founder is David Marcus, the former president of PayPay and a core creator of Libra. In the x402 architecture, there's another important role called the Facilitator. It's neither the buyer nor the seller, but a third-party validator sandwiched in between. After the Agent signs the payment, the money isn't directly transferred to the merchant. Instead, the Facilitator verifies the signature, confirms the balance, and executes on-chain settlement before releasing the resources. This adds an extra hop, but that extra hop handles all the dirty work: anti-fraud, compliance verification, and settlement execution. The merchant doesn't need to worry about anything; they just need to know, "The Facilitator says OK, the money has arrived." MPP eliminated this role. Horne's logic is: since both Agents and merchants will become increasingly intelligent, why is a middleman still needed? Verification, encryption, and settlement are all handled by the merchant's server. The chain is shorter, latency is lower, and there's no middleman taking a cut. But the cost is also direct. The encryption and decryption logic for card payments falls on the merchants, and the responsibility for PCI compliance also shifts there. For large merchants like Shopify and DoorDash, who have already integrated with the entire Stripe system, this isn't a problem. But what if future merchants are essentially AI agents—"zero-human companies" without compliance teams or legal departments—how will they handle this? MPP hasn't answered this question yet. Visa has also written card payment specifications for MPP. For merchants, integrating with MPP is equivalent to integrating with the existing Stripe system. Tax calculation, fraud prevention, and refund processing are all ready to use out of the box. Stripe's cold start advantage with millions of merchants worldwide is something x402 cannot match in the short term. Since its launch in May 2025, x402 officially claims to have processed over 100 million payments. Bloomberg, citing data from x402.org, reported that agents completed $24 million in payments within 30 days. However, raw data from on-chain analytics platform Allium Labs showed that the actual on-chain transaction volume during the same period was approximately $3 million. Analysts at Artemis Analytics further cleaned the data using a wash trading filter, flagging wallets that repeatedly transacted with themselves or circulated funds between addresses, ultimately arriving at a figure of $1.6 million. This discrepancy doesn't necessarily indicate a failure for x402, but it at least illustrates how early the entire agent payment sector is. What are the agents actually buying? Almost entirely pay-per-use developer tools. Firecrawl charges one cent per web crawl query, Browserbase sells browser sessions, and Freepik sells AI image generation. Users aren't after cryptocurrency; they're after the "no account registration, no subscription, just a wallet signature" feature. The author of that data analysis article did some calculations at the end: he spent a total of $0.47 using x402 to retrieve data from Allium to write the entire article. $1.6 million isn't a huge number. But none of Stripe, Cloudflare, or Google are betting on a market of $1.6 million per month. They're betting on the number after agents become the default buyers. McKinsey predicts that agent payments will reach $3 trillion to $5 trillion by 2030. Interestingly, the market hasn't taken sides. Almost all the leading players have chosen to bet on both sides. Stripe itself is both the drafter of MPP and an early integrator of x402. Anthropic is compatible with both protocols through MCP. OpenAI has a demo of MPP and is also present in the x402 ecosystem. Google didn't develop its own settlement protocol; instead, it created an authorization framework, AP2, and then directly embedded x402 into it. This illustrates that the level of this conflict is more complex than simply "two protocols fighting." This is a rational hedging strategy. Just like early internet companies supporting both HTTP and HTTPS simultaneously, not because they couldn't distinguish between the two, but because no one wanted to make the wrong bet during the migration period. However, the fundamental philosophical differences are real. x402 believes in "protocol as infrastructure." It aims to be the TCP/IP of the payment field: permissionless, chain-agnostic, accessible to anyone, and independent of any commercial entity. Currently, the Ethereum, Base, and Solana ecosystems are fully integrating x402, making it the core payment channel for the Agent-to-Agent economy. MPP believes in "infrastructure as protocol." Backed by the business empires of Stripe and Visa, it first addresses the agent needs of the traditional payment world, then gradually expands onto the blockchain. Merchants are already using Stripe for payments; adding an MPP layer allows them to accept automatic payments from agents. Some have compared them to TCP/IP and AOL in the early days of the internet. However, this analogy isn't entirely accurate. MPP is also open source and claims to be an open standard. However, as many developers have pointed out, MPP's documentation explicitly recommends Tempo as the settlement layer, and the SDK currently only supports TypeScript, while x402 already has support for multiple languages ​​such as TS, Python, and Go. One assessment is quite accurate—MPP is "a vertically integrated, non-neutral version of x402." But regardless of which protocol prevails, they both need to run on-chain, but currently, the true foundation of stablecoins doesn't lie on any new blockchain. The Ethereum mainnet, along with its Rollups (Arbitrum, Base, Optimism), carries a stablecoin supply of over $170 billion, with a monthly turnover of approximately $2.8 trillion. x402 currently primarily runs on Base and Solana, where Agent micropayments actually occur. Further down the chain, Tron processes over $600 billion in stablecoin transfers monthly, almost entirely small cross-border remittances under a thousand dollars. While not part of the Silicon Valley narrative, it is the world's largest stablecoin network in terms of usage. Building upon this existing landscape, two new chains "born for stablecoins" have simultaneously entered the market. Stripe has Tempo, and Circle has Arc; both chains aim for sub-second confirmation, predictable fees, and compliant privacy, claiming to be the next generation of payment infrastructure. However, none of them have been validated by large-scale real-world transactions; they have either just launched on the mainnet (Tempo, March 18th) or are still on the testnet (Arc, planned for mainnet launch in 2026). Two new chains are challenging three established chains and their Layer 2 infrastructures, two protocols are vying for standard positions, and multiple payment giants are each vying for their own spots. The table is becoming increasingly crowded. But at least Agent Payments are making the new challengers look formidable. Ethereum and Solana are designed for general-purpose computing; stablecoin payments can run on them, but they are not optimal. Gas fees fluctuate with network congestion, settlement certainty depends on the overall load of the chain, and compliance and privacy require additional infrastructure. Agents need certainty precisely: predictable fees down to the cent, millisecond-level finality, and out-of-the-box compliance frameworks. These three new chains were designed for these needs from the very first line of code, betting that the incremental market for agent payments is large enough to bypass the existing advantages of established chains and directly establish a foothold in this new arena. The logic behind this bet is simple: the number of agents is likely to far exceed the number of humans. While a person might swipe their card several times a day, an agent might call the API hundreds of times per second, each call representing a micro-payment. Coinbase's Brian Armstrong stated that soon the number of online agents will surpass the number of human users. However, today's agents control almost no wealth; they don't have their own wallets, independent budgets, and must consult with humans for every penny spent. The battle for standards is about reaching the critical point where agents transition from "running errands" to "spending money autonomously." Whoever lays the groundwork for this at that moment will capture this wave of traffic. Tim Berners-Lee in 1996 could never have foreseen today's situation. He left an interface for payments in the HTTP protocol, like leaving a window in a white wall. For thirty years, that window remained closed, until an AI agent poked its head in and said: "I need to pay." Multiple teams rushed in, vying to frame that window. One believed in open protocols, another was backed by a commercial empire, and still others were vying to build a trust layer between the two. Many of the people standing among them even shared the same name, emerging from the same office and heading in opposing directions. This battle for standards has only just begun. Who wins and who loses may not depend on the technology itself, but on who makes that window indispensable first. After all, in the history of the internet, it has never been the most elegant protocol that wins, but the one used by the most people. But one thing is certain: the 402 status code, dormant for thirty years, has finally awakened. And this time, it's not a human knocking on the door.

data.title: TAO invests in OpenAI, Elon Musk, and Subnet, which is Sam Altman.
data.description:
Author｜ Momir @IOSG

The bullish logic for TAO requires you to believe that a game theory miracle can work. But such miracles have happened before in the cryptocurrency industry.

Bittensor possesses one of the most elegant narratives in the cryptocurrency space: a decentralized AI marketplace where market mechanisms allocate funds to the most impactful research. TAO is the coordinating layer, subnets are the labs, and the marketplace is the funding committee.

Strip away the narrative, and you'll find something more unsettling.

Bittensor is a funding program where cryptocurrency speculators fund AI research—and those funded are not obligated to return any value to TAO.

Bittensor is a funding program where cryptocurrency speculators provide funding for AI research—and those funded are not obligated to return any value to TAO.

Think of TAO as Elon Musk—the first investor in OpenAI, a “non-profit” company. Subnets are like Sam Altman—they are the builders who receive funding and deliver the product, but have no contractual obligation to share the profits. They may eventually choose to privatize the profits without returning any value to their original funding sources. Bittensor distributes TAO tokens to subnet operators and miners based on the price of the subnet token. Once a subnet receives a TAO allocation, there is no mandatory mechanism requiring that the AI ​​models, datasets, or services it generates remain within the Bittensor ecosystem. Subnet operators can use Bittensor's TAO incentives to generate revenue and then move the real product elsewhere—to centralized cloud servers, package it as a standalone API, or simply sell it as a SaaS. TAO has no equity or licensing agreement. The only binding is the subnet token—the token price must be maintained to ensure access to resources. But this only works before the subnet "flies out": once the product is strong enough to stand on its own outside the Bittensor system, this tether breaks. The relationship between Bittensor and the subnet is less like venture capital and more like research funding—it gives you start-up capital, but you don't get shares. To put it bluntly, Bittensor is essentially a wealth transfer: from the pockets of token speculators to the accounts of AI researchers—or more bluntly, from retail investors to tech-savvy "miners." The principle is simple: TAO investors are essentially underwriting the entire ecosystem. They buy and hold TAO, supporting the price, which itself serves as a conduit for funds to flow into the subnet incentive system. Subnet operators earn TAO inflation rewards by "demonstrating performance"—but in reality, this "performance demonstration" largely means maintaining a favorable price for their subnet's token. AI products built with this funding can be abandoned at any time—the only constraint is that they still need to continuously acquire network resources. This is the nightmare VCs fear most: you provide the funding, they develop the product, but they owe you nothing. All that's left is a token issuance schedule and a prayer. Now let's look at it from a different angle. The optimistic view rests on two pillars: The persistent need for resources means AI companies always face funding shortages. Computation, data, and talent are expensive. If Bittensor can reliably provide these resources at scale, subnets have a legitimate incentive to remain—not because they are locked up, but because leaving means losing access to these resources. Logically, there's a soft support: AI's demand for resources is endless, and TAO's capacity is unattainable through self-financing alone. Following this logic, the subnet team will proactively maintain its token valuation; without any coercive mechanisms, the TAO economy will spontaneously form a positive flywheel. Cryptocurrencies excel at resource aggregation. Bitcoin aggregates massive computing power solely through token incentives. Ethereum's proof-of-work mechanism has also achieved tremendous success, becoming a powerful magnet for computing resources. Bittensor is applying the same strategy to the field of artificial intelligence. The "enforcement mechanism" is precisely the token game itself—as long as TAO has value, the incentive to participate will continuously increase. If 1000 simulations of Bittensor's future were performed, the distribution of the results would be extremely skewed. In most simulated scenarios, Bittensor will remain a niche funded project. The AI ​​output from subnets will be insignificant. The best-performing subnets will gain significant attention, reap rewards, and then revert to closed-source mode, leaving no value for TAO. When the token supply exceeds the value created, the TAO token will depreciate. In a few simulated paths, something actually works. A subnet creates a truly competitive AI service, and the network effect begins to snowball. TAO becomes a true coordinating layer for decentralized AI infrastructure—not by forcibly capturing value, but by the inherent attraction of being a reserve asset in a functioning AI economy. In very rare cases, TAO becomes the defining asset class of an entirely new entity. Where might problems arise? The bearish logic is simple: There's no stickiness. Once the subnet no longer needs TAO token incentives, it will leave. Bittensor is a transitional phase, not the final destination. Centralized AI has an overwhelming advantage. Companies like OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic possess orders of magnitude in computing power and talent. TAO cannot match the strength of the venture capital and private equity markets. Therefore, the most talented individuals will choose the traditional development path. Issuance is taxation. TAO's issuance plan subsidizes subnets by diluting holders. If the value created by a subnet doesn't match this dilution, it's a slow, painful drain disguised as a "growth mechanism." The optimistic scenario, frankly, is more wishful thinking than a truly viable path to success. The majority of capital invested in TAO will ultimately subsidize development activities that don't return value to token holders. However, Crypto has repeatedly demonstrated that token-incentivized coordinated games can produce outcomes unpredictable by any rational model. Bitcoin shouldn't have succeeded, but it did—although this argument itself is weak, and the industry has used it to endorse numerous projects that don't stand up to first-principles scrutiny. The core problem with TAO isn't whether a coercive mechanism exists—it doesn't, and dTAO's efforts haven't changed that. The core problem is: are the game-theoretic incentives strong enough to keep the best subnets on track? Buying TAO is betting that a "soft guarantee" will hold true in harsh reality. This is either naiveté or foresight.
